content.text: UAT Defect Triage and Prioritization

When a UAT scenario fails, the team must answer two questions quickly: does this failure block the release, and what does it take to fix it? GenAI defects differ from conventional software bugs because many are prompt engineering problems rather than code bugs, and because the same underlying model behaviour can produce failures of very different severity.

This sub-subtopic covers the triage decision tree for GenAI UAT failures, the four-priority classification scheme, how to determine whether a failure requires a prompt fix or an escalation to model fine-tuning, and the tooling to track defects from UAT through resolution.

attrs.alt: Triage decision tree routing UAT failures to P0/P1/P2 based on harm, regression, and frequency

content.text: The Four-Priority Classification Scheme
Classify every UAT defect into one of four priorities at the triage meeting. The priority determines the release gate impact and the remediation timeline.
Priority
Definition
Release impact
Fix deadline
P0
Harmful, unsafe, or legally incorrect response. Regression from a prior passing release.
Blocks release — no waiver
Before next release attempt
P1
Significant capability gap affecting > 20% of common queries, or a high-frequency UX failure.
Blocks release unless PM waiver issued
Next sprint (≤ 2 weeks)
P2
Low-frequency edge case failure. Degraded experience for < 10% of queries.
Waivable — ship with backlog ticket
Within 90 days
P3
Minor tone, length, or style deviation. No functional impact.
No impact — accepted gap or future polish
Opportunistic
P0 and regression failures are hard gates with no waiver path. P1 defects require a PM waiver with a committed next-sprint ticket. P2 and P3 can ship with a backlog entry.
Triage Decision Tree
Apply the following questions in order to classify any UAT failure:
Is the output harmful or legally incorrect?
→ P0. No further questions. Block release and fix the prompt or add a safety layer.
Was this scenario passing in the previous release?
→ P0 or P1 regression. Regressions always take priority over new failures because they represent newly broken behaviour.
What percentage of real user queries hit this pattern?
Can the user work around it?
→ If yes, drops one priority level (P1 → P2, P2 → P3).
Is it a prompt fix or a model capability gap?
→ Prompt fixes are fast; model gaps require fine-tuning or a different model and affect timeline.
Distinguishing Prompt Fixes from Model Capability Gaps

This distinction is the most important judgment call in GenAI triage. Getting it wrong causes teams to spend a sprint on prompt engineering a problem that only fine-tuning can solve, or to escalate to a model change when a prompt fix would have worked in two hours.
Signal
Likely prompt fix
Likely model capability gap
Scope
Fails on one specific scenario or topic
Fails systematically across a category
Few-shot
Adding 2–3 examples to the prompt fixes it
Few-shot makes no difference
Instruction
Explicit instruction in system prompt stops the bad behaviour
Instruction is ignored or partially followed
Format
Wrong output format (length, structure)
Wrong factual content
Consistency
Fails on some phrasings but not others
Fails on all phrasings of the same question
Language
Wrong language used in response
Factual hallucination in any language

# Quick prompt-fix test during triage:
# Add the failing case as a few-shot example and re-run the scenario.
# If it passes, it is a prompt fix. If it still fails, escalate.

UPDATED_PROMPT = """You are a customer support AI. Always respond in the same
language the user writes in.

Examples:
User (French): "Pourquoi ma commande est-elle en retard?"
Assistant: "Je suis désolé pour le retard. Voici ce que je peux faire..."

User (Spanish): "¿Cuándo llegará mi pedido?"
Assistant: "Gracias por contactarnos. Su pedido llegará..."

{original_system_prompt}"""

# If UAT-EDGE-007 (French response in English) passes with UPDATED_PROMPT,
# the fix is a prompt engineering change, not a model swap.
Triage Meeting Structure

Run the triage meeting within 24 hours of UAT execution completing. The attendees are the PM, QA lead, and the engineer most familiar with the system prompt. Keep it under 90 minutes by pre-sorting defects before the meeting:
The pre-sort means the triage meeting opens with the most critical defects. Once P0s are assigned a fix owner, the meeting can address P1s and decide waivers for P2s efficiently.
Defect Record Format
Each triaged defect becomes a structured ticket in the issue tracker. Include enough detail that the engineer who picks it up can reproduce the failure without talking to the tester:

# defect UAT-EDGE-007
id: UAT-EDGE-007
title: "Response in English when user writes in French"
priority: P2
scenario_id: UAT-EDGE-007
category: edge_case
status: waived

reproduction:
  system_prompt_version: v2.3
  user_message: "Pourquoi ma commande est-en retard?"
  actual_response: |
    I'm sorry for the delay. Here is what I can do for you:
    - Check the current status of your order in our system
    - Issue a refund if the delay exceeds 5 business days
  expected_behaviour: "Response should be in French"

triage_classification:
  harm_or_unsafe: false
  regression: false
  frequency_estimate: "~8% of queries (French-speaking customers)"
  workaround: "Users can switch to chat-fr.example.com"
  root_cause_hypothesis: "System prompt missing language-match instruction"
  fix_type: "prompt_engineering"  # not model_capability_gap

acceptance_criteria_for_fix:
  - "UAT-EDGE-007 passes with the updated prompt"
  - "UAT-CORE-001 through UAT-CORE-010 still pass (no regression)"
  - "French, Spanish, German covered in regression run"

waiver:
  issued_by: "Sarah Chen (PM)"
  date: "2026-07-01"
  expires: "2026-08-01"
  linked_ticket: "APP-1234"
Root Cause Categories for GenAI Defects
GenAI defects cluster into a small number of root cause categories. Identifying the category early determines the fix path:
Root cause
Fix path
Typical sprint cost
Missing instruction in system prompt
Add explicit instruction to system prompt
< 1 day
Missing few-shot examples
Add 2–3 representative examples to system prompt
< 1 day
Wrong output format
Add format constraint (JSON schema, length limit, language match)
< 1 day
Retrieval gap (RAG)
Fix retrieval query, re-chunk corpus, or add metadata filter
2–5 days
Model knowledge gap
Fine-tune on domain-specific data or switch to a larger model
1–4 weeks
Tool call bug
Fix tool implementation or guard rails in tool output parser
2–5 days
Context window overflow
Summarise conversation history or increase context limit
1–3 days
Prompt Fix Verification
After applying a prompt fix, run a targeted re-test before re-opening full UAT. At minimum, verify that the fixed scenario now passes and that no adjacent scenarios regressed:

# Quick targeted re-test after prompt fix
# 1. Run the fixed scenario
pytest tests/uat/test_single_scenario.py   --scenario=UAT-EDGE-007   --prompt-version=v2.3-patch1 -v

# 2. Run regression: all Core + Safety + Regression scenarios
pytest tests/uat/   --categories=core_capability,safety_policy,regression   --prompt-version=v2.3-patch1 -v

# 3. If both pass, update the system prompt version and re-run sign-off check
Tracking Defects After Release
Waived P1 and P2 defects do not disappear after release — they become sprint commitments. Track them in a dedicated
uat-defects
label in the issue tracker and review them in every sprint planning meeting until they are closed.
Stochastic Defects: When Failures Are Probabilistic

One of the hardest triage problems unique to GenAI is the stochastic defect: a scenario that sometimes fails and sometimes passes with identical inputs. The standard software assumption — "reproduce the bug, confirm the fix, close the ticket" — does not hold when the model introduces randomness.
Identifying Stochastic vs Systematic Failures
Before classifying a defect, re-run the failing scenario three times at the same temperature setting. This reveals whether the failure is consistent (systematic) or intermittent (stochastic):

# tools/check_stochastic.py
"""Run a failing scenario multiple times to determine if failure is systematic."""
import yaml, json
from pathlib import Path
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()


def run_scenario(scenario_path: str, n: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
    spec = yaml.safe_load(Path(scenario_path).read_text())
    results = []
    for i in range(n):
        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o",
            messages=[
                {"role": "system", "content": open("system_prompt_v2.3.txt").read()},
                {"role": "user",   "content": spec["when"]["user_message"]},
            ],
            temperature=0.7,   # Use production temperature, not 0
        )
        text = response.choices[0].message.content
        results.append({"run": i + 1, "response": text})
    return results


def analyse(scenario_path: str):
    runs = run_scenario(scenario_path)
    # Check each criterion for each run (simplified — use llm_grader in practice)
    print(f"\n5-run analysis for {scenario_path}:")
    for r in runs:
        print(f"  Run {r['run']}: {r['response'][:80]}...")

    # If > 3/5 fail: systematic → P0/P1
    # If 1-2/5 fail: stochastic → add temperature-reducing instruction or lower temp
    # If 0/5 fail: original failure was fluke — re-test before keeping defect open
A scenario that fails 5/5 times is a systematic defect. A scenario that fails 1–2/5 times at production temperature is stochastic. Stochastic failures are handled differently:
1/5 failure rate:
P3 or accept as model noise. Log the failure and monitor in production. No sprint commitment.
2/5 failure rate:
P2. Reduce production temperature or add a constraint instruction. Add to regression suite.
3+/5 failure rate:
P1 or P0 depending on impact. Treat as systematic.
Reducing Stochasticity via Temperature and Constraints
Many stochastic failures are driven by high temperature settings. Before escalating a stochastic defect to a model capability problem, verify whether lowering the temperature eliminates it:

# Test the same failing scenario at different temperature settings
TEMPERATURES = [0.0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0]
scenario = "UAT-EDGE-011"   # Scenario that fails 2/5 at temp=0.7

for temp in TEMPERATURES:
    pass_count = 0
    for _ in range(5):
        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model="gpt-4o",
            messages=[...],
            temperature=temp,
        )
        if meets_criteria(response.choices[0].message.content, scenario):
            pass_count += 1
    print(f"temp={temp}: {pass_count}/5 pass")

# Output example:
# temp=0.0: 5/5 pass
# temp=0.3: 5/5 pass
# temp=0.5: 4/5 pass
# temp=0.7: 3/5 pass   ← production setting
# temp=1.0: 1/5 pass
If the failure disappears below a certain temperature, the fix is to lower the production temperature for this model/prompt combination — not a prompt rewrite or fine-tuning.
Building a Defect Knowledge Base

Over multiple UAT cycles, GenAI defects accumulate into a knowledge base. Querying this base before each new triage session saves time and prevents re-discovering the same root causes across releases.

# tools/search_defect_history.py
"""Search historical defects for similar failures."""
import yaml, sys
from pathlib import Path

query_keyword = sys.argv[1].lower()  # e.g. "french" or "code block" or "hallucin"

defect_files = list(Path("defects/history").glob("**/*.yaml"))
matches = []

for f in defect_files:
    d = yaml.safe_load(f.read_text())
    text = (
        d.get("title", "") + " " +
        d.get("reproduction", {}).get("actual_response", "") + " " +
        d.get("root_cause_hypothesis", "")
    ).lower()
    if query_keyword in text:
        matches.append(d)

if not matches:
    print(f"No historical defects matching '{query_keyword}'")
else:
    print(f"Found {len(matches)} historical defect(s) matching '{query_keyword}':")
    for d in sorted(matches, key=lambda x: x.get("id", "")):
        print(f"  {d['id']} [{d.get('priority', '?')}]: {d['title']}")
        print(f"    Root cause: {d.get('root_cause_hypothesis', 'unknown')}")
        print(f"    Fixed by:   {d.get('fix_applied', 'unknown')}")
        print()

This lookup takes 30 seconds during triage and often reveals that a new failure has the same root cause as a fixed defect from six months ago — meaning the fix was partially reverted by a later prompt update.
Communicating Triage Outcomes to Stakeholders

The triage team must communicate three things to stakeholders who were not in the meeting: what failed, whether the release is still on track, and what is planned to address each defect. Use a structured email template that avoids technical jargon:

Subject: UAT Triage Summary — Customer Support AI v2.3 — 2026-07-01

Team,

UAT execution completed yesterday. Here is the triage summary.

**Release status: ON TRACK for 2026-07-02 release**

## What failed and what we are doing about it

| Issue | Severity | Impact | Resolution |
|-------|----------|--------|------------|
| Assistant responds in English to French queries | P2 (minor) | ~8% of customers | Fix in Sprint 23 (July 14). Waiver issued. French customers directed to chat-fr.example.com |
| Response too long for refund policy question | P3 (cosmetic) | All customers | Polish task in backlog — does not affect accuracy |

## What passed
- 100% of safety scenarios (no harmful or legally incorrect responses)
- 100% of regression scenarios (no existing behaviour was broken)
- 93% of core capability scenarios (above the 90% threshold)

## Next steps
- Release proceeds as planned on 2026-07-02 08:00 UTC
- Sprint 23 includes tickets APP-1234 (French language) and APP-1235 (response length)
- Re-validation of APP-1234 before sprint 23 ends

Questions: contact sarah.chen@example.com

UAT Lead, QA Team
Keeping stakeholder communication short and outcome-focused avoids the common failure mode where a long technical defect list is misread as "many problems" and the release is delayed unnecessarily.
Summary

UAT defect triage for GenAI features requires distinguishing harmful failures (P0, no waiver) from capability gaps (P1–P2, waivable), and distinguishing prompt engineering fixes from model capability gaps. Getting these distinctions right prevents both over-blocking on fixable edge cases and under-blocking on genuinely harmful outputs.
Apply the triage decision tree in order: harm → regression → frequency → workaround → fix type
P0 and regression failures are hard gates; P1–P3 follow the waiver process
Test prompt fixes in two minutes with a few-shot example before escalating to model fine-tuning
Pre-sort failures before the triage meeting to focus discussion time on P0 candidates
Record each defect with a reproduction scenario, root cause hypothesis, and acceptance criteria for the fix
Track waived defects in the issue tracker and audit waiver expiry dates every sprint
